report: The patient is lying supine with eyes closed and displays initial restlessness, followed by facial twitching. A blank stare ensues, accompanied by rightward head version and right arm extension. The patient then lifts their head slightly and opens their eyes, while beginning to move their arms asymmetrically—initially raising one arm while keeping the other extended. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity with rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs, including tonic stiffening of the left arm and asynchronous, irregular clonic jerks that vary in intensity and are more pronounced on the left side. Arm movements include flexion and extension bilaterally, evolving into sustained rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms. Throughout all phases, the patient remains unresponsive.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, then transitions to bilateral tonic-clonic activity characterized by rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and tonic stiffening of the left arm, accompanied by right arm extension. Repetitive hand-to-head gestures are observed, indicating potential automatisms. This is followed by bilateral arm flexion and extension, progressing into generalized tonic-clonic activity characterized by rhythmic, synchronous, and repetitive jerks of the arms and legs. The episode includes periods of tonic stiffening with brief relaxation and emergence of slight clonic movements in the left limb. Throughout the entire sequence, the patient remains unresponsive, consistent with a generalized seizure.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and asymmetric motor activity involving both arms. The left arm shows rhythmic, repetitive flexion and extension, while the right arm demonstrates similar but less pronounced movements with concurrent extension. There is no evidence of clonic jerks or progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity during the observed period. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rhythmic, asynchronous movements of both arms. The left arm shows repetitive flexion and extension, while the right arm remains relatively still but occasionally exhibits minor jerks. The patient's facial expression remains unchanged throughout the sequence.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rhythmic, asynchronous jerking movements of the arms and legs. The movements appear to alternate between flexion and extension, suggesting a possible focal onset that may evolve into a generalized seizure pattern. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the observed sequence.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. Subsequently, there is a transition to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic jerks. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the sequence.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by a brief period of smiling and talking. Subsequently, the patient's facial expression changes to one of discomfort, with eyes closed and mouth slightly open. The patient remains mostly still throughout, with minimal movement of the arms and hands.

The patient appears alert and responsive, lying in bed with a bandage on their head. They are holding a laptop, opening and closing it while speaking. At one point, the patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight head turn to the left. There are no observable signs of seizure activity such as head version, arm flexion or extension, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or automatisms. The patient's movements are deliberate and controlled, with minimal movement aside from adjusting their grip on the laptop, indicating no active seizure behavior.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight facial twitching. There is no significant tonic or clonic activity observed during the sequence. The patient remains largely still, with minimal spontaneous movement.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight facial twitching, with minimal body movement primarily involving minor facial and head adjustments. This progresses to rhythmic, asynchronous jerking movements of the arms and torso that alternate between flexion and extension, suggesting a possible focal onset that may evolve into a generalized seizure pattern. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the observed sequence and largely still except for the described motor activity.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension in one sequence, accompanied by rhythmic, asynchronous jerking of the limbs, suggesting a tonic-clonic seizure. In another instance, the patient displays a blank stare with subtle lip smacking, without head version or significant limb movement, remaining relatively still. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed episodes.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by a gradual onset of rightward head version and right arm extension. The patient's left arm remains relatively still, indicating asymmetrical movement. There is no evidence of clonic jerks or further progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity during the observed period. The patient appears unresponsive throughout the sequence.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight facial twitching. There is minimal upper body movement, including occasional minor hand tremors and slight hand and arm adjustments. The patient remains largely still and unresponsive throughout the sequence.

The patient is lying down, wearing a hospital gown and a cap. Initially, the patient appears calm and is engaged in reading a book, with no evidence of seizure activity such as blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion or extension, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, automatisms, or asynchronous movements. The patient remains responsive during this period. Subsequently, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic jaw movements and lip smacking, with no apparent head version or limb movement. During this time, the patient becomes unresponsive.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, during which they appear unresponsive and remain relatively still with minimal spontaneous movement. This state of immobility is characterized by no visible tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, head version, arm flexion or extension, or significant changes in posture. In some segments, the eyes appear closed, and there are no observable motor activities. Subtle motor phenomena are noted in several instances, including slight facial twitching, jaw movements, lip smacking, facial grimacing, and subtle head movements—sometimes specifically to the left. These movements occur without generalized tonic or clonic activity and are not associated with changes in posture or limb involvement. At other times, the patient exhibits rhythmic, bilateral, and synchronous clonic jerks involving the arms, legs, and head, consistent with a generalized seizure. These episodes include both tonic stiffening and clonic jerking of moderate intensity and occur while the patient remains unresponsive. Automatisms such as lip smacking and facial grimacing are observed during one segment, along with asynchronous facial twitching and jaw movements, without limb involvement. In contrast, there are periods where the patient appears alert and responsive, sitting upright with hands clasped, calm and engaged, with no signs of seizure activity or motor abnormalities. During these intervals, there is no blank stare, head version, tonic-clonic activity, or automatisms.

The patient exhibits a blank stare and remains unresponsive throughout the observed sequences. There is no overt movement or signs of seizure activity in some segments, with the patient lying supine, eyes closed, and maintaining a static posture without changes in muscle tone, limb positioning, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks. In other segments, the patient demonstrates clear epileptic activity: intermittent rightward head version is noted, along with unilateral arm flexion and extension, specifically involving the right arm and progressing to rightward head version and right arm extension. This is followed by bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic, synchronous, and repetitive jerking of both arms and legs, consistent with a generalized seizure. Additionally, there are episodes of rhythmic clonic jerks isolated to the left leg, indicating a focal motor seizure. No automatisms or significant postural changes are observed outside of the described events.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by a brief period of apparent stillness. In one sequence, this is followed by a rightward head turn and right arm extension with concurrent left arm flexion, consistent with fencer posturing, indicating asymmetrical movement; the patient remains unresponsive and there is no progression to bilateral clonic activity during this episode. In another instance, the initial stillness is succeeded by a sudden onset of rhythmic, bilateral clonic jerks involving both arms and legs, with the patient remaining unresponsive throughout. A separate observation notes a similar sequence beginning with a blank stare and stillness, then progressing to noticeable rhythmic, bilateral clonic jerks affecting all limbs, again without responsiveness. Additionally, an episode featuring rightward head version and right arm extension with minimal movement in the left arm is documented, showing no clonic jerking or evolution into generalized tonic-clonic activity, and the patient remains unresponsive throughout.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rhythmic, asynchronous jerking movements involving the arms and legs. The movements are primarily clonic in nature and appear to alternate between flexion and extension, with the left arm showing more pronounced jerking compared to the right. The arms move in an uncoordinated fashion during the clonic phase. The episode may begin with focal features, such as rightward head version and right arm extension, and progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity involving both upper and lower extremities. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the entire sequence.

The patient appears to be engaged in a calm activity, drawing on a piece of paper. There are no observable signs of seizure activity such as blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion or extension, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks. The patient remains focused on the task at hand without any indication of altered consciousness or involuntary movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight arm adjustments. There is no evidence of tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or automatisms. The patient remains relatively still throughout the sequence.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight facial twitching. Repetitive hand movements near the face, including touching the mouth and chin, are observed, with the right arm showing slight, irregular movements while the left arm remains relatively still. There is no evidence of tonic-clonic activity or automatisms throughout the sequence. The patient remains relatively still overall, with minimal spontaneous movement, but appears responsive and engaged in subtle, purposeful actions.

The patient is seated upright in a hospital setting, wearing a surgical cap and a hospital gown, and appears to be manipulating a small device near their chest area, possibly related to medical monitoring or treatment. The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight facial twitching; however, there are no observable signs of seizure activity such as lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks. The patient remains focused on the task at hand without any indication of altered consciousness or involuntary movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rhythmic, repetitive movements of the left hand over the chest area. The movements appear synchronous and involve both hands, suggesting a possible focal motor seizure. There is no evidence of tonic-clonic activity or automatisms.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rhythmic, jerking movements involving the arms and torso. The movements are clonic in nature and appear asynchronous, with alternating flexion and extension; the left arm demonstrates more pronounced jerking compared to the right. Both arms show symmetric involvement at times, but overall the motor activity is asymmetric. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient appears alert and responsive at times, holding a phone to their ear and remaining engaged and communicative throughout portions of the observation, with no observable signs of seizure activity such as blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion or extension, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks during these intervals. During other intervals, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking in the absence of head version or limb movement, remaining relatively still and unresponsive, suggesting possible early seizure activity without overt tonic-clonic manifestations. Episodes of rhythmic, repetitive clonic movements are observed involving the left hand, which flexes and extends while holding a remote control, with the patient remaining unresponsive. Additional episodes involve rhythmic, asynchronous jerking movements of both arms, more pronounced on the left with flexion, while the right remains relatively extended; the patient is unresponsive during these events. In more generalized episodes, the patient displays a blank stare accompanied by rhythmic, asynchronous clonic jerking of the arms and head, with uncoordinated limb movements and no responsiveness throughout the episode.

The patient is lying supine with a bandage on their head and exhibits a blank stare. They are holding a device near their face, possibly a breathing apparatus, and making rhythmic, repetitive hand movements around it. There is no evidence of tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or other seizure-related activity, and the patient remains relatively still throughout the sequence, with minimal change in facial expression.

The patient lies supine with chest exposed, wearing either a white cap or a white bandage on the head. The right hand rests on the chest in some instances, while no specific hand positioning is noted in others. The patient exhibits recurrent episodes characterized by a blank stare, often followed by subtle or rhythmic lip smacking and/or jaw movements. In several episodes, this is accompanied by rhythmic, asynchronous clonic jerking of both arms, more pronounced in the left arm, which shows increased flexion compared to the right arm that remains relatively extended. There is no tonic stiffening or generalized tonic-clonic activity observed. The left arm may remain extended at times, while slight flexion is noted in the right arm during certain sequences. No significant head movement or version is consistently present, though one episode includes brief rightward head version and subtle right arm flexion. Automatisms are generally absent, though intermittent facial expression changes suggest possible automatisms or vocalizations in one instance. The patient remains unresponsive throughout most episodes, with minimal spontaneous movement or slight adjustments in hand positioning. One distinct sequence involves deliberate actions including opening the mouth wide, raising a hand to the chest, and manipulating a tablet with both hands—movements that appear controlled and purposeful, without any features suggestive of seizure activity such as clonic jerks, tonic stiffening, or automatisms. Across all observations, the patient appears calm and stable when not exhibiting episodic behaviors, with no overt signs of seizure activity in baseline periods.

The patient exhibits a blank stare with eyes partially open and no spontaneous eye movements. The mouth is slightly agape, and there is no visible movement of the limbs or torso in most episodes. Several observations note rhythmic jaw movements and lip smacking, consistent with automatisms, while one episode includes only a brief period of lip smacking without other motor features. In one instance, the patient shows a rightward head turn accompanied by slight right arm extension, without tonic-clonic activity or additional automatisms. Across all episodes, the patient remains unresponsive throughout, with minimal changes in facial expression. These findings collectively suggest recurrent focal seizures, with most not spreading beyond the initial focus, though one event may represent a focal seizure with impaired awareness and early lateralizing signs.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by one or more of the following: subtle lip smacking and repetitive chewing movements; rhythmic, repetitive movements of the hands and fingers; or rhythmic, bilateral clonic jerks of the arms, which may extend to the torso. The movements are synchronous and repetitive, consistent with a generalized seizure. No significant head movement, head version, or tonic-clonic activity is observed. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

The patient is initially seated in a chair, wearing a white cap and a blue shirt, appearing engaged in a conversation while holding a phone to their ear with their left hand. The facial expression is relatively neutral with occasional slight movements of the mouth and head, maintaining a steady posture and continuing to interact with the phone. During the observation, the patient exhibits episodes characterized by a blank stare and intermittent or subtle lip smacking. There is no evidence of head version, arm flexion or extension, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or other seizure-related motor activity. The patient remains relatively still throughout the observation period.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by various manifestations including subtle lip smacking, facial twitching, and head movements. There is no apparent limb movement in some episodes, while others involve rhythmic clonic jerks of both arms characterized by synchronous repetitive flexion and extension. Additional observations include rightward head version with right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic, asynchronous shaking of the limbs. Tonic stiffening is also noted, with slight elevation of both arms and generalized body tensing preceding the clonic phase. Despite these motor phenomena, there is no clear evidence of tonic stiffening or clonic jerks in certain segments, though facial expressions suggest potential seizure activity. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed episodes.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by a sudden onset of tonic stiffening with arms slightly raised and clenched fists, accompanied by rigid posture. This is followed by clonic jerks involving rhythmic, involuntary movements primarily of the upper extremities and progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. The episode includes rightward head version and right arm extension prior to generalized involvement. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the event and shows no overt signs of seizure activity before or after the episode, such as automatisms or postictal movements, remaining motionless during periods of observation outside the seizure.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by various motor manifestations indicative of seizure activity. Automatisms are observed, including repetitive lip smacking and hand movements near the face, without significant tonic or clonic activity; during these episodes, the patient remains seated and intermittently interacts with a laptop. In other instances, the automatisms progress to increased motor activity, with the patient leaning forward and eventually bending over, remaining unresponsive throughout. Some episodes feature sudden, forceful movements, including bilateral arm raising and wide mouth opening, accompanied by significant muscle tension and rigidity consistent with generalized tonic activity, without clear lateralization. Other events are characterized by rhythmic, synchronous, bilateral clonic jerks involving the arms, head, and torso, consistent with generalized seizures, with preserved unresponsiveness. Asynchronous, bilateral jerking of the arms and legs is also observed, with uncoordinated clonic movements suggesting possible focal onset evolving into bilateral involvement. One distinct sequence begins with a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, transitioning into bilateral tonic-clonic activity with rhythmic jerking. Across all episodes, the patient remains unresponsive.

The patient exhibits a sudden onset of symptoms beginning with a blank stare, followed by a sudden upward gaze and head extension. This is accompanied by tonic stiffening with a brief upward arching of the body and arms, including bilateral arm flexion and extension. The tonic phase rapidly transitions into clonic jerking, characterized by rhythmic, asynchronous shaking of the limbs, more pronounced in the left arm compared to the right. A subtle shift in posture under the blanket may precede these events, sometimes accompanied by a slight turning of the head to the side and extension of one arm, with movements progressing into rhythmic jerking. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all phases of the episode.

The patient exhibits a sudden onset of seizure activity characterized by a blank stare, followed by rhythmic, synchronous, bilateral clonic jerks involving both arms and legs. The seizure begins with either a brief period of immobility or initial focal features such as rightward head version and right arm extension, suggesting a possible focal onset that progresses to generalized tonic-clonic activity. There is an evolution in the seizure pattern, with asynchronous jerking observed initially in some segments, progressing to widespread, repetitive, and synchronous bilateral movements. The seizure increases in intensity and the patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient is observed in various positions, primarily lying down or seated, wearing a hospital gown and a head covering. In several segments, the patient remains still with no observable signs of seizure activity, including absence of blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion or extension, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, automatisms, or asynchronous movements. The hands are positioned near the chest or abdomen during lying phases, and at times are clasped together or engaged with a handheld device when seated, indicating alertness and focus. In specific instances, the patient exhibits a blank stare accompanied by brief unresponsiveness, without progression to more severe motor manifestations or lateralization. Repetitive hand movements near the face, including rubbing and wiping actions, are noted during episodes of unresponsiveness. These movements appear deliberate but are interspersed with periods of stillness, consistent with possible seizure-related automatisms. No significant tonic or clonic activity is observed throughout any segment. Overall, multiple observations show no evidence of active seizure behavior, while a subset indicates subtle clinical features suggestive of non-convulsive seizure activity, particularly characterized by blank stare and manual automatisms. The patient maintains a neutral posture when seated, appears calm, and shows no changes in facial expression or body movement indicative of seizure onset or evolution in other segments.

The patient is lying supine, wearing a white cap, and appears to be holding or engaged with a tablet device at certain points during the observation. Initially, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by a brief period of focused attention on the tablet, with no signs of seizure activity during this time. There are no observable signs of lip smacking, head version, arm flexion or extension, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks during periods of alertness or engagement. However, the patient subsequently exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic, symmetrical clonic jerks of both arms involving alternating flexion and extension. This progresses to an episode of tonic stiffening with arms raised above the head, followed by clonic jerks affecting both arms. Automatisms, including repetitive hand movements, are observed during the episode. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the seizure activity and relatively still outside of the motor manifestations.

The patient exhibits a blank stare and remains unresponsive throughout each episode. In several instances, this is followed by rhythmic, bilateral clonic jerks of the arms and legs that are synchronous and generalized without clear lateralization. In one instance, the movements were asynchronous but remained primarily clonic in nature, with no tonic stiffening observed. Additionally, there is an episode characterized solely by a blank stare with no visible movement or change in posture—eyes appear closed, and there is an overall state of immobility without any seizure-related motor activity. Another episode features rightward head version with right arm extension, without progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity or clonic jerking.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by variable clinical manifestations indicating different seizure semiologies. In some episodes, there is a gradual onset of rightward head version and right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic activity with rhythmic jerking movements. In other instances, the seizure manifests as rhythmic, synchronous clonic jerks of the arms and torso, consistent with a generalized seizure pattern. Alternatively, the patient displays asynchronous clonic jerking of the arms and head without clear tonic stiffening. Some sequences involve only a brief period of unresponsiveness or subtle signs such as slight head turning (rightward or leftward), minimal postural adjustments, or mild arm movements, with no significant tonic or clonic activity observed. One episode includes a transition from initial confusion or disorientation to unresponsiveness with closed eyes, suggesting progression into a more generalized phase. Throughout all episodes, the patient remains unresponsive. No automatisms or repetitive rhythmic movements are noted in certain sequences, while others clearly demonstrate generalized motor involvement.

The patient is lying in a hospital bed, wearing a plaid shirt and checkered pants, with a bandage wrapped around their head. The patient exhibits a blank stare and intermittent lip smacking, followed by repetitive head movements and facial twitching, including noticeable facial grimacing. The patient's eyes appear to roll back slightly during the episode, and they remain unresponsive throughout the sequence. There is no evidence of tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, automatisms, or other seizure-related movements. The patient's left hand is raised to their face, touching their cheek and ear area, while the right hand rests on their lap or near the abdomen. Posture and behavior suggest possible discomfort or pain, but no seizure activity is observed beyond the initial described symptoms.

The patient is observed in various positions—seated in a chair, seated in a hospital bed, and lying down—wearing either a white beanie or a white headband (or cap) and consistently dressed in a plaid shirt. The patient appears alert, oriented, and engaged in conversation or attentive listening, with no observable signs of seizure activity throughout the sequences. Specifically, there are no manifestations of blank stare, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion/extension, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, automatisms, or asynchronous movements. At times, the patient exhibits repetitive hand movements such as clenching and unclenching fists and rubbing hands together, with focused attention on their hands. In other instances, the patient uses hand gestures while speaking and remains responsive. The hands are frequently clasped together, and posture occasionally shows tension, suggesting possible discomfort or distress, though without accompanying motor seizure activity. One observation notes subtle signs of discomfort or distress despite maintained alertness, with still posture and no significant motor activity, potentially indicating a pre-ictal or interictal state. Overall, the patient remains relatively still across all observations, with stable posture and no notable changes in movement or behavior suggestive of active seizure episodes.

The patient exhibits a blank stare and lies still with minimal spontaneous movement, showing no overt signs of seizure activity. There is no evidence of tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, head version, arm flexion or extension, or other seizure-related movements. The head remains stationary, and the facial expression is neutral, with only subtle head movements and slight facial twitching noted in one observation. The patient appears unresponsive throughout the observed period, with no visible automatisms or asynchronous movements, indicating a lack of active seizure behavior.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by a gradual onset of rightward head version and right arm extension. As the seizure progresses, there is a transition to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs. Additionally, the patient demonstrates rhythmic, asynchronous facial twitching and jaw movements. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by orofacial movements including lip smacking and rhythmic jaw motions. This progresses to bilateral arm involvement with flexion and extension, evolving into generalized tonic-clonic activity characterized by synchronous, rhythmic clonic jerks of all limbs. The movements may initially present as asynchronous but become bilateral and repetitive, consistent with a generalized seizure. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient exhibits a sudden onset of generalized tonic-clonic seizure activity, beginning with a brief period of apparent confusion or disorientation and a blank stare, followed by rapid progression to full-body convulsions. The episode is characterized by rhythmic, forceful, and synchronous bilateral clonic jerks involving the arms, legs, head, and torso. The movements are repetitive and indicate a generalized seizure, during which the patient remains unresponsive throughout.

The patient lies still in bed with eyes closed, wearing a blue RAMS shirt, a white headband, or a head wrap (variously described), and maintains a neutral facial expression throughout the observation. There are no visible signs of seizure activity such as tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or automatisms. The patient remains motionless during prolonged periods, with minimal spontaneous movements noted in some segments. Intermittently, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking, without significant head movement or arm flexion/extension. These episodes occur while the patient remains relatively still overall.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle lip smacking in some sequences, and in others by subtle head movements or a brief rightward head turn. Subtle facial twitching is noted across multiple observations, occasionally accompanied by slight opening of the mouth. In several instances, there is a gradual or noticeable onset of rightward head version with concurrent right arm extension. One segment shows a period of stillness followed by rhythmic, asynchronous shaking of both arms and legs, indicative of clonic jerks. There is no significant tonic stiffening or overt tonic-clonic activity observed in most sequences, and no consistent arm flexion or extension except as noted. The patient remains relatively still throughout all observed periods, with minimal spontaneous movement; upper body motion is limited primarily to minor hand and arm motions where present. Automatisms such as lip smacking are observed, but no other prominent automatisms are reported. During most episodes, the patient appears unresponsive, though in one instance the patient is described as responsive with intermittent eye contact. Eyes are closed at rest in one segment with no signs of seizure activity.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic, asynchronous jerking movements of both arms, progressing from subtle to more pronounced and involving both upper extremities. Additionally, subtle head movements and slight facial twitching are observed, without tonic stiffening or prominent clonic jerks. In another episode, the patient displays bilateral and synchronous rhythmic clonic jerks of the arms and legs, consistent with a generalized seizure. Throughout all episodes, the patient remains unresponsive and relatively still, with minimal spontaneous movement.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic, bilateral clonic jerks involving the arms and legs in multiple episodes, with synchronous and repetitive movements indicating generalized seizure activity. In one episode, the clonic jerks primarily involve the face and upper body, also appearing synchronous and bilateral. Another episode features a gradual onset of tonic stiffening with right arm extension and left arm flexion, resembling a fencer posture, progressing to more pronounced clonic jerks that spread to both arms and torso. A distinct event begins with brief restlessness, followed by sudden extension of the left arm and head turning to the left, then a pause before the patient returns to a relaxed state with eyes closed, suggesting an isolated motor event without progression to generalized seizure activity. The patient remains unresponsive during all episodes.

The patient is lying supine with eyes closed, showing no initial movement. A blank stare ensues, followed by sudden leftward head turning and a brief period of stillness. The patient then begins to sit up, raising their upper body while keeping the legs extended, suggesting an initial focal seizure with evolution into a more complex motor pattern. Subsequently, rightward head version and right arm extension occur, transitioning into bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by synchronous, rhythmic jerks of the arms and legs. Throughout the episode, the patient remains unresponsive, with no spontaneous activity aside from seizure-related movements. Repetitive lip smacking and subtle head movements are noted in one phase, but no tonic stiffening is observed prior to the clonic activity. The progression from focal onset with asymmetric features to bilateral involvement indicates potential generalization of seizure activity.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by a brief period of unresponsiveness. During one sequence, there is a rightward head version and subtle right arm flexion; otherwise, the patient remains relatively still with minimal movement aside from slight adjustments in hand positioning. No tonic stiffening or clonic jerks are observed, and there is no clear lateralization or evolution into more severe seizure activity.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight facial twitching, including rhythmic jaw movements and lip smacking. There is no significant tonic or clonic activity, head version, or limb movement observed during the sequence. Minimal upper limb movement is noted, primarily consisting of minor hand fidgeting and occasional finger tapping. The patient remains largely still and unresponsive throughout the observation.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight facial twitching. There is minimal arm movement, with occasional asynchronous jerking of both arms and legs that alternate between flexion and extension; the left arm demonstrates more pronounced repetitive, rhythmic flexion and extension compared to the right, which remains relatively still. The right arm and leg remain largely immobile throughout. The patient's eyes appear open but unresponsive, with no change in facial expression, indicating a lack of awareness and consistent unresponsiveness during the episode. Movements do not progress to involve both sides of the body symmetrically.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by variable motor manifestations indicating seizure activity. Initial signs include intermittent head movements, slight arm adjustments, and mild discomfort or restlessness, without clear tonic stiffening or clonic jerks in some instances. In other episodes, the seizure begins with head deviation—either to the right or left—and may involve tonic stiffening of one arm (left or right) along with asymmetric posturing, such as right arm extension or asymmetric clonic movements with greater left arm flexion. Progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity is observed in several segments, characterized by synchronous, rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs, consistent with generalized seizure activity. Clonic features also appear asynchronously in some cases, accompanied by facial grimacing. Throughout all episodes, the patient remains unresponsive.

The patient is seated in a chair, wearing a hospital gown and a bandage on their head, and appears alert at baseline with preserved interaction with objects on a tray, including deliberate use of utensils while eating. Periodic episodes are characterized by a blank stare accompanied by unresponsiveness, subtle head movements, slight shifts in gaze direction, and minor involuntary facial twitching; these episodes lack tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or automatisms, suggesting possible absence or subtle non-motor seizure activity. During one episode, the patient exhibits repetitive hand movements near the face, chest, and abdomen, suggestive of automatisms, without tonic-clonic activity, though motor control and awareness appear partially preserved. Another distinct sequence begins with a blank stare and leftward head tilt, followed by asymmetric motor signs: tonic stiffening and extension of the left arm and leg, right arm flexion, and left arm extension resembling fencer posturing. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity with synchronous, rhythmic jerking of all extremities, during which the patient remains unresponsive. Additional observations include isolated tonic stiffening of the left arm without clonic activity, as well as generalized bilateral clonic jerks following a blank stare, with moderate-intensity, synchronous movements of the arms and legs, consistent with generalized seizure activity. No focal motor features such as head version are clearly observed in all episodes, and there is no evidence of prolonged automatisms or post-ictal agitation across the sequences.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight facial twitching. There is no significant tonic or clonic activity observed during the sequence, including no apparent tonic stiffening or clonic jerks. The patient remains relatively still throughout, with minimal spontaneous movement aside from minor facial expressions.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by either subtle lip smacking or rhythmic clonic jerks of both arms and legs. In some episodes, the jerking is symmetrical and synchronous, indicating a generalized seizure, while in others it is asynchronous, with no clear tonic stiffening observed. A gradual onset of rightward head version accompanied by right arm extension is also noted in certain instances. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed sequences.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle lip smacking, slight head turning to the left, and minimal spontaneous movement. There is occasional asynchronous twitching and rhythmic, clonic jerking of both arms, more pronounced on the left. Slight arm flexion is noted without clear evidence of tonic stiffening. The patient remains largely unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient appears to be lying still with eyes closed, exhibiting a blank stare and showing no overt signs of seizure activity such as tonic stiffening, automatisms, head version, arm flexion, or extension. There is no evidence of coordinated motor activity.
